subject: Careers
topic: The Social Dilemma: Algorithms, Attention, and Cognitive Bias
grade_subject_fit: Fits Grade 8 Careers through self-awareness, digital influence, decision-making, and responsible online choices.
unit_context: Follows a short mini-unit on cognitive bias.
assignment_purpose: Students use the documentary as a case study for persuasive design, digital influence, and cognitive bias.
final_evidence_target: A short exit response that names one design choice, one likely bias or vulnerability, and one healthier response or redesign.
student_task_flow: Use the packet during the film.
Connect examples to persuasive design and cognitive bias.
Finish with a short post-film response.
success_criteria: I can identify persuasive design choices from the film.
I can connect a film example to a bias or vulnerability.
I can propose a realistic healthier response or redesign.
supports_scaffolds: Restricted response types to reduce note-taking load.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Pause briefly and allow partner talk before harder blanks.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Students complete the companion independently, then debrief.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Students judge whether business model, algorithm design, or user habits matter most and justify their view.

checkpoint_release_logic: Use quick partner checks if students lose the thread of the documentary argument.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep returning to cognitive bias language.
Film = one perspective worth testing, not automatic truth.
likely_misconceptions: Students may assume the film says all technology is bad.
Students may treat algorithms as neutral math instead of systems shaped by human choices.
Students may think only individual willpower matters, missing the role of design and accountability.
assessment_focus: Accuracy of key ideas from the film
Connection to cognitive bias or vulnerability

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide
teacher_guide.overview: Use The Social Dilemma as a case study in how digital systems shape attention, emotion, and decision-making.
teacher_guide.project_prompt: Students watch the documentary, track persuasive design examples, connect them to cognitive bias or behavioral vulnerability, and propose healthier responses or redesigns.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Students should be able to name a design feature, connect it to a bias or vulnerability, and propose a realistic response or redesign.
teacher_guide.big_idea: Digital platforms do not just host choices; they shape choices.
learning_goals: Use cognitive bias vocabulary during viewing.
Reflect on effects on well-being, beliefs, and choices.
Propose realistic changes in habits, norms, or design.
materials: Netflix access to The Social Dilemma

timing.activity: Launch, reconnect to cognitive bias, explain the packet.

teacher_notes: Timestamps are approximate and can shift slightly depending on playback version.
Preview the film for fit with your class and school context before use.
Push students to separate documentary claim, documentary evidence, and their own judgment.
Answer key — Opening claim: The companies mostly compete for user attention.
Answer key — Business model blank: behavior.

Answer key — Platform goals matching: Engagement -> Keep users clicking, scrolling, and staying longer; Growth -> Keep users returning and bringing in more users; Advertising -> Turn user attention and data into revenue.
Answer key — Persuasive design features: Infinite scroll and notifications encourage repeated checking.
Answer key — Mental health blank: anxiety.
Answer key — Misinformation: False or extreme content often spreads because it gets stronger engagement.

Answer key — Bias matching: opinion -> Confirmation bias; everyone is talking about it -> Social proof; shocking story -> Availability heuristic; might miss something -> Loss aversion / fear of missing out.
Answer key — Possible responses: More humane design and stronger accountability.
Answer key — Post-film planner: Answers vary, but should name a design choice, a plausible bias or vulnerability, and a realistic response or redesign.

model.sample: A platform can feel useful and still be designed to pull me back more often than I intended. A healthier redesign would slow the pressure to react instantly.

task_sheet.title: The Social Dilemma Video Companion
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use this companion before, during, and after the documentary.
instructions: Answer in the spaces provided while you watch.
Use matching, multiple choice, and fill-in-the-blank only.

tasks.label: Before Watching / Matching Bank
tasks.prompt: Match each item to the best description.

right_items: Feeling the pain of losing something more strongly than the pleasure of gaining something
Judging what is common or likely based on what comes to mind fastest
Following what seems popular, approved, or socially rewarded
Noticing and trusting information that already fits what you believe

render_hints.heading: Opening claim
choice_options: The companies mostly compete for user attention
The companies mostly sell school supplies
The companies mainly want users offline
The companies are neutral public services

render_hints.heading: Business model
blank_prompts: The film argues that platforms make money by predicting and influencing user ________.

tasks.prompt: Match each item to the best description.

right_items: Keep users returning and bringing in more users
Turn user attention and data into revenue
Keep users clicking, scrolling, and staying longer

render_hints.heading: Persuasive design features
choice_options: Infinite scroll and notifications encourage repeated checking
Locked paper notebooks encourage repeated checking

render_hints.heading: Mental health effects
blank_prompts: The film connects heavy social comparison to rising ________ and lower well-being for some teens.

tasks.prompt: Match each item to the best description.

left_items: Seeing more posts that already fit your opinion
Clicking because everyone is talking about it
Refusing to stop scrolling because you might miss something
right_items: Loss aversion / fear of missing out

render_hints.heading: Possible responses
choice_options: More humane design and stronger accountability
More persuasive tricks and more outrage

render_hints.heading: Post-film planner
blank_prompts: One design choice that stood out to me was ________.
A bias or vulnerability it may use is ________.
One healthier response or redesign could be ________.

embedded_supports: If you miss an answer, leave it and catch the next one.
Use the timestamp as a guide, not as a reason to panic if playback is slightly off.
Focus on the film's argument, not on writing full sentences during every section.
success_criteria: I completed the companion using the key ideas from the film.
I connected at least one film example to a cognitive bias or vulnerability.
I suggested one healthier response or redesign.

quick_check_items: I identified at least one persuasive design choice.
I connected at least one example to bias or vulnerability.
I suggested one healthier response or redesign.

exit_ticket.title: Optional Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: Name one platform design choice from the film. Explain which bias or vulnerability it may use and one healthier response, norm, or redesign.
success_criteria: I named one design choice clearly.
I connected it to a bias or vulnerability in thinking or behavior.
I proposed one realistic response or redesign.
answer_key: Answers will vary. Strong responses name a specific design choice, connect it to a plausible bias or behavioral vulnerability, and propose a realistic action or redesign.
